revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine]][[ja:星宮社]] {{Expand Japanese|星宮社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine | native_name = 星宮社 | image = Hoshimiya-sya01.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.088424|136.934714}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|it|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|ja|天津甕星|mni|ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|ru|Амацумикабоси|th|อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|tr|Amatsu Mikaboşi|zh|天津甕星|qid=Q4740636|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine'''|星宮社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Owari Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Q134917287|ltq=Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|lt=Atsuta Jingū|ar|أتسوتا جينغو|az|Atsuta məbədi|cs|Svatyně Acuta|de|Atsuta-jingū|en|Atsuta Shrine|eu|Atsuta santutegia|fa|معبد آتسوتا|fr|Atsuta-jingū|hu|Acutai nagyszentély|id|Kuil Atsuta|it|Santuario di Atsuta|ja|熱田神宮|ko|아쓰타 신궁|la|Atsuta Templum|or|ଆତ୍ସୁତା ଶାଳ|pl|Atsuta-jingū|pt|Santuário de Atsuta|simple|Atsuta Shrine|th|ศาลเจ้าอัตสึตะ|tr|Atsuta Tapınağı|uk|Святилище Ацута|zh|熱田神宮|qid=Q482065|1=Atsuta Shrine}} * {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} * 103 ** Series ordinal: 104 ** P155: {{ill|Hioki Shrine (Nagoya)|lt=Hioki Shrine (Nagoya)|ja|日置神社 (名古屋市)|qid=Q11510248|1=Hioki Shrine (Nagoya)}} ** P155: Province|ja|尾張国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} ** Series ordinal: 103 ** P155: {{ill|Q11510248|ltq=Q11510248|qid=Q11510248|1=Hioki Shrine (Nagoya)}} ** P156: district|ceb|Aichi-gun|de|Aichi-gun|en|Aichi District|es|Distrito de Aichi (Aichi)|fa|شهرستان آیچی، آیچی|fr|District d'Aichi|hu|Aicsi körzet|id|Distrik Aichi, Aichi|it|Distretto di Aichi|ja|愛知郡 (愛知県)|ko|아이치군|pl|Powiat Aichi|ru|Айти (уезд)|tl|Distrito ng Aichi, Aichi|uk|Повіт Айті|vi|Aichi (huyện)|zh|愛知郡 (愛知縣)|qid=Q552687|1=Aichi District}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|35.088424|136.934714}} == image (P18) == * Hoshimiya-sya01.jpg == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Q4740636|ltq=Q4740636|qid=Q4740636|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|ltq=Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == country (P17) == * Bikéyah|ny|Japan|oc|Japon|olo|Japounii|om|Jaappaan|or|ଜାପାନ|os|Япон|pag|Hapon|pam|Hapon|pap|Hapon|pa|ਜਪਾਨ|pcd|Japon|pcm|Japan|pdc|Japan|pfl|Japan|pl|Japonia|plnews|Kategoria:Japonia|plquote|Japonia|plvoyage|Japonia|pms|Giapon|pnb|جاپان|pnt|Ιαπωνία|ps|جاپان|pt|Japão|ptnews|Categoria:Japão|ptquote|Japão|ptvoyage|Japão|pwn|Dripun|qu|Hapun|rm|Giapun|rmy|Japan|rn|Yapon|roa_rup|Japonia|roa_tara|Giappone|ro|Japonia|ronews|Categorie:Japonia|rovoyage|Japonia|rue|Японія|ru|Япония|runews|Категория:Япония|ruvoyage|Япония|rw|Ubuyapani|sah|Дьоппуон|sat|ᱡᱟᱯᱟᱱ|sa|जपान्|scn|Giappuni|sco|Japan|sc|Giapone|sd|جاپان|se|Jáhpan|sg|Zapöo|shn|မိူင်းၵျႃႇပၢၼ်ႇ|sh|Japan|simple|Japan|si|ජපානය|skr|جاپان|sk|Japonsko|sl|Japonska|smn|Jaapaan|sm|Iapani|sn|Japani|so|Jabaan|sq|Japonia|sqnews|Kategoria:Japoni|srn|Japan|sr|Јапан|srnews|Категорија:Јапан|srquote|Јапан|ss|IJapani|stq|Japan|st|Japane|su|Jepang|sv|Japan|svnews|Kategori:Japan|svvoyage|Japan|sw|Japani|syl|ꠎꠣꠙꠣꠘ|szl|Japůńijo|szy|Zipun|ta|யப்பான்|tanews|பகுப்பு:ஜப்பான்|tay|Gipun|tcy|ಜಪಾನ್|tdd|ᥛᥫᥒᥰ ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5180005001657 == Commons category (P373) == * Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Shimotsuchikamano Shrine|lt=Shimotsuchikamano Shrine|qid=Q135039548|1=Shimotsuchikamano Shrine}} * {{ill|Q135039546|ltq=Q135039546|qid=Q135039546|1=Kamutsuchikamano Shrine}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県名古屋市南区本星崎町字宮西620', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県名古屋市熱田区神宮', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine}} [[JA:星宮社]] {{wikidata link|Q11512854}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Hoshimiyasha |Image = [[File:Hoshimiya-sya01.jpg|250px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|qid=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|qid=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Minami Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155286|qid=Q1155286|lt=Minami Ward|1=Minami-ku, Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Honhoshizaki Town|Q38276297|qid=Q38276297|1=Motohoshizaki-chō}}Miyanishi 620 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|35|5|18|N|136|56|5|E|display=it}} |Enshrined God = {{ill|lt=Tianjin Wengxing|Q4740636|qid=Q4740636|lt=Tianjin Wengxing God|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi}} |Shakaku = old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Foundation = (tradition) {{ill|lt=Emperor Jumei|Q329761|qid=Q329761|1=Emperor Jomei}} August 9th year of Hei-roki ({{ill|lt=637 years|Q33294|qid=Q33294|1=637}}) |Main shrine = |Alternative name = Hoshizakisha
Hoshi no Miya
Hoshimiya Daimyojin
Hoshimiya
Hoshisha |Regular festival = 1st Sunday in October and the day before |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Hoshimiyasha|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{Location map|Nagoya|label=Hoshimiya|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[Image: Hoshimiya Onogi-jinja Ino-watashi(Owari Meisho Zue).jpg |thumb|250px|right|``Owari Meisho Zue'']] '''Hoshimiyasha'' (Hoshimiyasha, Hoshimiyasha) is {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|qid=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|qid=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Minami Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155286|qid=Q1155286|lt=Minami Ward|1=Minami-ku, Nagoya}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Honhoshizaki Town|Q38276297|qid=Q38276297|1=Motohoshizaki-chō}}. === Overview === It is an old shrine that is said to have been founded in the {{ill|lt=Asuka period|Q210688|qid=Q210688|1=Asuka period}}, and the name ``Hoshizaki'' appears in an old poem from the end of the Heian period, so it seems to have been worshiped since {{ill|lt=ancient times|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}}. The plaque on the shrine building says ``Hoshizaki Shrine'', the ``{{ill|lt=Owari Meisho Zue|Q11465290|qid=Q11465290|1=Owari meisho zue}}'' written in the late Edo period says ``Hoshi no Miya'', and the ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Owari Makiyuki|1=UNKNOWN}}'' says ``Hoshimiya Daimyojin''.After the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|qid=Q184963}}, many {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shiogama|1=UNKNOWN}} were dotted around the area, so Hoshimiya Shrine was named in {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|qid=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}{{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|qid=Q552687|lt=Aichi District|1=Aichi District}} A theory spread that it was ``Kamichigama Shrine'' or ``Shimochigama Shrine'' {{Sfn|Nagoya Local Culture Association|1980|pp=7-13}}. However, in recent years, Aichi-gun {{ill|lt=Aichi-gun (Aichi Prefecture)#Go|Q552687|qid=Q552687|lt=Chigama-go|1=Aichi District}} from the south of the current {{ill|lt=Nakamura Ward|Q780521|qid=Q780521|1=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}} to the north of {{ill|lt=Nakagawa Ward|Q1155226|qid=Q1155226|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}} The theory that it is compared to Nagoya Prefecture is now considered to be the most likely, and the theory that our company is Shigechigama Shrine has been denied {{Sfn|Nagoya Folk Culture Association|1980|pp=7-13}}. Shimochigama Shrine, which is a separate shrine within the precincts, is not mentioned in the ``{{ill|lt=Kanbun Village Memorandum|Q137766629|qid=Q137766629|1=Kanbunmuramuraoboegaki}}'' established in the early Edo period, and it is presumed that it was enshrined after the mid-Edo period {{Sfn|Nagoya Folk Culture Association|1980|pp=7-13}}. === History === === Founding === {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} According to the Owari Makiyukiki written in 1822, it was founded ``at the time of Emperor Jomei's visit to Yushinden.'' The entry for Hoshizakisho (Minamino Village) mentions the ``Minamino Meteorite,'' and says that it may have come to be called ``Hoshi-sha (Hoshimiya-sha)'' because this meteorite fell. It is thought that it may have been founded by the Yamaguchi clan, a descendant of the Ouchi clan, who valued the faith of K|Myoken Bodhisattva|Q10960623}}. However, since the Minamino meteorite, which is the material for consideration, fell in the early Edo period, the chronology is reversed and contradictory. Owari Makiyuki also notes that this shrine may have been enshrined in the early modern period for another reason: ``The name book of the Aichi gods shows that samurai children visited this shrine.'' {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} The Owarishi of 1844 cites an editorial that states that in the 9th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jomei|Q329761|qid=Q329761|1=Emperor Jomei}} ({{ill|lt=637 years|Q33294|qid=Q33294|1=637}}), seven stars descended from the sky and there was an oracle, so a shrine was built in this area, which was the old Chiba village. The book suggests that the landowner deity that had been enshrined in this area since ancient times was not Seimiya Shrine, but Jochichigama Shrine, and that this shrine transformed into Myoken worship in the 9th year of Emperor Jomei's reign. {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year (1913) Aichi County Magazine states that in September 9th year of Emperor Jomei, {{ill|lt=oracle|Q217123|qid=Q217123|1=Oracle}} ``built the first star shrine in Chiga no Sato.''The name of Hoshimiya Shrine is not listed in the domestic Shinto name book of Owari Province. At the time of its founding, Hoshimiya Shrine was built near what is now Kasadera Elementary School (Hoshizaki Castle Ruins), but there is a theory that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}} built {{ill|lt=Hoshizaki Castle|Q2969408|qid=Q2969408|1=Hoshizaki Castle}}. The current site of the shrine was at the edge of Hoshizaki Cape on the Kasadera Plateau . In ancient times, the Kasadera Plateau was known as Matsuko Island (Matsuko Island, Matsuko Island). It is said that there was a mansion of {{ill|lt=Kokuzō Owari|Q11465294|qid=Q11465294|1=Owari no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}'s {{ill|lt=Nodomeyome|Q97706258|qid=Q97706258|1=Otoyo no mikoto}} (the enshrined deity of Kamichi Gama Shrine) in a place called ``Kunizo Owari|Q11465294}}. === Legends about stars and meteorites === {{Infobox Meteorite |Name = Minamino Meteor
Minamino |Image = Stone Monument of Shiotanosato Hoshizaki, Minamino Minami Ward Nagoya 2022.jpg |Image_width = |Image_caption = Stone monument at the fall site |Type = |Class = |Group = |Structural_classification = |Composition = |Shock = |Weathering = |Country = {{JPN}} |Region = {{ill|lt=Owari County|Q514506|qid=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}{{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|qid=Q552687|lt=Aichi District|1=Aichi District}}{{ill|lt=Hoshizaki Village|Q21654585|qid=Q21654585|lt=Minamino Village|1=Hoshizaki}}
Current: {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|qid=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|qid=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Minami Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155286|qid=Q1155286|lt=Minami Ward|1=Minami-ku, Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Minamino (Nagoya City)|Q51698443|qid=Q51698443|lt=Minamino|1=Minamino}} |Lat_Long = |Observed_fall = |Fall_date = {{ill|lt=1632|Q6865|qid=Q6865|1=1632}}{{ill|lt=September 27th|Q2884|qid=Q2884|1=September 27}}
{{ill|lt=Japanese calendar|Q5372133|qid=Q5372133|1=Japanese year}}: {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kanei 9|1=Kan'ei}}{{ill|lt=August 14th (lunar calendar)|Q840163|qid=Q840163|lt=August 14th|1=August 14 (Lunisolar)}} |Found_date = |TKW = 1040g |Image2 = |Image2_caption = }} [[Image:Yobitsugi-jinja Shrine Haiden, Hoshizaki Minami Ward Nagoya 2022.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Kanjosha, which enshrines the Minamino meteorite.
(Hoshizaki, Minami Ward, Nagoya City, January 2022)]] Records and legends about stars and meteorites have existed in the Hoshizaki area since ancient times. There are the following theories regarding the place name Hoshizaki. * Owari Makiyuki is written by {{ill|lt=Shohei (Japan)|Q1324874|qid=Q1324874|lt=Johei|1=Jōhei}}5 years (935), when the {{ill|lt=Johei-Tenkei Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|qid=Q2975322|lt=Taira no Masakado Rebellion|1=Tengyō no Ran}} occurred, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Imperial Order|1=UNKNOWN}} records the tradition that he prayed for {{ill|lt=Shofuku|Q24897750|qid=Q24897750|1=Shofuku}} at Atsuta Daimyojin (Atsuta Shrine). Another story states that in the ``Atsuta annual event,'' the mikoshi from Atsuta Shrine was handed over to Seimiya Shrine. When the shrine family prayed, seven stars shone at Hoshimiya Shrine, so the village came to be called Hoshizaki.* Owarishi says, ``The place name of Koreru Hoshizaki is based on the Seisha Shrine, and even though it seems like the first hundred poems of the poem called Hoshizaki, in that period it was mostly Oshiha Karururuya,'' and says that Seishi Shrine is the origin of the place name. The Aichi County Magazine also makes the following observation: ``Couldn't the place name Hoshizaki come about because it was owned by Hoshimiya Company?'' * {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|qid=Q1136866|1=Mainichi Shimbun}}'s ``Town name origin record'' (1954) states that the place names Hoshizaki and Narumi arose from the fact that the morning star fell on the inlet on May 24, 1205, {{ill|lt=Motokyu|Q1194276|qid=Q1194276|1=Genkyū}}. This tradition is based on Jizo Engi at Shugen Jizo-in Temple, and is also mentioned in the section on Yamazaki Village in the Owari Makiyuki. On the same day, the heavens and earth suddenly trembled, the sea rumbled, and many stars shone like thunder. However, when the villagers went out to sea, there were no traces left. From this time, ``Narumi'' began to be written as ``Narumi,'' and the place where the morning star descended began to be called ``Hoshizaki.''As for reliable records, {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}9 {{jalink|August 14th} (Lunar calendar)|Q840163|August 14th}} ({{ill|lt=1632|Q6865|qid=Q6865|1=1632}}{{ill|lt=September 27th|Q2884|qid=Q2884|1=September 27}}), there is a "Minamino meteorite" that fell in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Minamino Village|1=UNKNOWN}}, which was a village next to Honchi Village. The situation at that time is depicted in the appendix ``Ojida no Shimizu'' of the Owari Meisho Zue. The meteorite was stored in a private house as a ``star stone'', but in 12 years ({{ill|lt=1829|Q7572|qid=Q7572|1=1829}}), it was donated to Yobitsugisha Shrine in the same village, and became its {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}}. The wooden box containing the meteorite has the words ``spirit stone'' written on the front. On August 15, 1976, Sadao Murayama of {{ill|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science|Q74940|qid=Q74940|1=National Museum of Nature and Science}} identified it as a meteorite and named it the "Minamino Meteorite." Currently, it is the second oldest meteorite in Japan, after the {{ill|lt=Nogata meteorite|Q1995438|qid=Q1995438|1=Nōgata meteorite}}, for which the age of the meteorite is known. There is a legend that a meteorite fell in this area in the 8th and 13th centuries. Part of it is thought to refer to the above-mentioned traditions from the 5th year of Johei (935) and the 2nd year of Genkyu (1205). Please note that Seimiya Shrine is not the site of a meteorite fall or that it is enshrined as a divine object. === Tradition about Taira no Masakado's prayer === At Kasadera, there are some ruins related to the tradition of the prayer for Taira no Masakado's chofu, which is mentioned in the Owari Makigyoki. * Kagutsuchi Shrine (Akiba Shrine), located to the west of Kasadera Elementary School, is the place where the mikoshi of Atsuta Shrine was passed over, and has the old name of Mikoshiyama (Mikoshiyama). * Kasadera Shichisho Shrine is said to have been a place where the Seven Gods of Atsuta were summoned to pray for the peace of the world, as there was a spiritual experience in the prayer of Taira no Masakado at Seimiya Shrine. * At Mt. Tanpachi, north of Kasadera Elementary School, there is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira no Masakado's head mound|1=UNKNOWN}}. Tanpachizan is not a historical site, but a park maintained and donated by {{ill|lt=Tanpachiro Ishikawa|Q28122554|qid=Q28122554|1=Tanpachiro Ishikawa}}, but the company's editorial confirms that it is a sacred place for Kasadera Shichisho Shrine. === History === * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jomei|Q329761|qid=Q329761|1=Emperor Jomei}}9th year ({{ill|lt=637th year|Q33294|qid=Q33294|1=637}}) - Founded. * {{ill|lt=Johei (Japan)|Q1324874|qid=Q1324874|lt=Johei|1=Jōhei}} 5th year (935) - Atsuta Shrine shrine family prayed for the end of Taira no Masakado's rebellion at Mikoshiyama. * {{ill|lt=Motohisa|Q1194276|qid=Q1194276|1=Genkyū}}May 24, 2012 (1205) - The star descends on the inlet. * {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}August 14, 9 ({{ill|lt=1632|Q6865|qid=Q6865|1=1632}}) - Minamino meteorite falls on Minamino Village. === Enshrined God === === Tianjin Yongxing God === The Aichi County Journal states that there are two deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|Q478504|qid=Q478504|lt=Koku no Tokodate no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} and Kakase Otokogami. Kakasei Otokogami is another name for {{ill|lt=Amatsu Onsei|Q4740636|qid=Q4740636|lt=Amatsu Onsei God|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi}}. Tianjin Wenxing God is the god of stars. He is also the ``Matsurowanu God'' who resisted the two war gods of {{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchijin|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|lt=Futunushi|UNKNOWN|lt=Keitsusujin}} to the end during the {{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchijin|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|lt=Futunushi|UNKNOWN|lt=Keitsusujin}}. is the god of war and the god of protection, military luck, and long life. {{ill|lt=Omika Shrine|Q60990714|qid=Q60990714|1=Ōmika Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Hitachi City|Q633922|qid=Q633922|1=Hitachi, Ibaraki}} There is a legend that it was subdued by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatebazuchi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (the enshrined deity of {{ill|lt=Shizu Shrine|Q11663648|qid=Q11663648|1=Shizu Shrine}}) in |Q1055353}}, and was sealed in a soul stone in the same place.The Mitarai Pond in front of the shrine is called ``Shichimen Pond'' because during the prayer for Taira no Masakado, people washed their faces so stained with blood that their eyebrows and eyes were barely visible. There is also a legend that ``seven pillars'' {{ill|lt=navigation marker|Q98272210|qid=Q98272210|lt=marker|1=Sea mark}} were erected for ships traveling to and from Otsu, southwest of Honchi Village. The {{ill|lt=lantern|Q12012294|qid=Q12012294|1=Stone lantern}} in the shrine grounds has the ``Seven Star Crest'' engraved on it. In addition to the ``seven stars'' that appear in the legend of Taira no Masakado Chofu, numbers such as seven faces, seven pillars, and seven star crests suggest that Seishimiya Shrine was recognized from an early stage as a shrine based on the Myoken faith. === Precinct Company === The Kanbun Memorandum (Memorandum of Kanbun Villages) states that there is one guardian deity, ``Hoshimiya, Tennozan, and two shrines.'' The Owari Makiyuki records that there are five shrines: Setsusha Sochi Gama Shrine, Shimo Chi Gama Shrine, Shinmei Tenno Soden, Inari Akiba Soden, and Reisha. The Owarishi records that there are seven shrines: Kamichigamasya, Shimochigamasya, Shinmeisha Tennosoden, Reisha, Inarisha Akibasoden, Akibasha, and Konpirasha. In the illustration of ``Hoshi no Miya'' in {{ill|lt=Owari Famous Places Picture Book|Q11465290|qid=Q11465290|1=Owari meisho zue}}, from the left side by head office, ``Konpira, Shinmei, Head Office, Inari, Akiha, Rei-sha'' are annotated, and the two u on the hill behind them are annotated with ``Inatsuwokinano-sha''. There are currently nine shrines in the precincts, as listed below. *Setsusha Kamichigama Shrine * Setsusha Shimochigama Shrine * Hakusansha * Kotohirasha * Tennosha Shinmeisha Hall * Kagudosha * Kyutsuchisha * Shrine * Heroisha === Festival === * Regular festival: First Sunday of October and the day before (Honji festival) === About Shimochigama Shrine === [[Image:Kamichikama-sha Shrine & Shimochikama-sha Shrine, Miyanishi Motohoshizaki-cho Minami Ward Nagoya 2021.jpg|thumb|Kamichigama Shrine (image right) / Shimochigama Shrine (image left) (August 2021)]] === Enshrined God === ==== Nodomeyo (Kamichigamama Shrine) ==== Mashiki Toju no Mikoto, Ina Tochi Old Man (Shimochigama Shrine). On the mountaintop behind the main shrine, there are two shrines, Kamichigama Shrine and Shimochigama Shrine, which enshrine the two parent deities {{ill|lt=Tateine ​​Tane-no-Mikoto|Q11486691|qid=Q11486691|1=Takeinadane}} and {{ill|lt=Miyazahime|Q11455216|qid=Q11455216|lt=Miyazanohime-no-Mikoto|1=Miyazu-hime}}. The two pillars, as {{ill|lt=Owari Kunizo|Q11465294|qid=Q11465294|1=Owari no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, set up a castle in Hidaka (present-day Otaka, the site of {{ill|lt=Hikami Aneko Shrine|Q11549548|qid=Q11549548|1=Hikami Anego Shrine}}) in the southeast, and their descendants {{ill|lt=Owari clan|Q11465311|qid=Q11465311|1=Owari clan}} served as the Daiguji family of Atsuta Shrine. Owarishi and Owari enshrine the two deities enshrined at the Shimosha as Mashiki Katana no Mikoto and Ina Tochiroo. The Owari Makiyuki records that there is a small shrine dedicated to ``Ina Tochio'' at the peak of Hoshimiya Shrine. The Owari Meisho Zue depicts ``Inatsuwokinano-sha'' on the top of the mountain behind the main shrine. According to the explanation in Minami Ward, Chigama Shrine enshrines the old man Inatsuchi, but it does not explicitly say whether it is one of the upper or lower shrines.According to the Minami Ward commentary, Old Man Ina Tochi is the person who introduced {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=salt production|1=UNKNOWN}} to the area. Owari Makiyuki records that he was the first person to bring the technique of baking solid salt in a clay oven to this area, and that he was the ``ancestor god of clay ovens'' that was enshrined to reward his efforts. Owarishi says that the old man Ina Tochi is a divine name that honors the person who brought the technique of burning sea salt using Shiogama to this area. According to the {{ill|lt=kiki|Q842308|qid=Q842308|1=Kiki (Shinto)}}, the name should have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shiotsuchiroo|1=UNKNOWN}}, but the name Ina Tochiroo is used as proof that he inherited the divine name from before the Middle Ages. This area used to be the coastline, and salt production was popular. === Shikinai Chigama Shrine === Kamichigama Shrine and Shimochigama Shrine are small shrines (shikinai shrines) listed in Aichi District, Owari Province in the Engishiki Shikimyocho. Chigama-jinja Shrine in Hoshimiya-sha Setsusha, along with Chigama-jinja Shrine in Atsuta-jingu Setsusha, is a non-sha of Chigama-jinja Shrine within this ceremony. There is an opinion that Hoshimiya Shrine's Chigama Shrine is the former shrine of Atsuta Shrine's Chigama Shrine. Although Owarishi declined, saying, ``I continue to write exhaustively with some speculation,'' he added the following considerations. * The reason why the portable shrine of Atsuta Shrine was handed over to Seishimiya Shrine for the prayer for Taira no Masakado's shofu is that the two shrines had a deep relationship from that time, similar to that of the shrine outside the shrine and the main shrine. In later generations, the reason why Shimochigama Shrine was moved to the market town of Atsuta Shrine (then Gendayu Shrine, now Kamichigama Shrine, Atsuta Shrine Precincts Setsusha) and Hatayacho (at that time Kitayu Shrine, now Shimochigama Shrine, Atsuta Shrine Precincts Setsusha) is said to be because of their deep relationship. * ``Aichi-gun Chiba-go'' in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|1=UNKNOWN}} is the old place name of Hoshizaki Seven Villages, and it is incorrect to call Atsuta Chiba-sho in early modern times. It is said that the reason why Shimochigama Shrine in Atsuta was called Gendayu-sha and Kitayu-sha until early modern times is because it was not ``Chigama'' (Chigata). Furthermore, the fact that the lower shrine is located closer to the main shrine, contrary to the hierarchy of the two shrines, is evidence that the shrine was moved from another location. Furthermore, {{ill|lt=Big Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149|qid=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}} compares Hoshizaki to Sakura-go rather than Senbaku-go, despite the quotation from Owarishi. * It is said that in ancient times, the Shimochigama Shrine was enshrined in two separate locations, the upper and lower, of the Hoshizaki Seven Villages in Chiba-go.{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Shinto Name Book|Q11571092|qid=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}} says about Shimochigama Shrine, ``Shimochigama Shrine was also located in a place called Shimochigo in Kamiko, and later moved here. He wrote, "My mother-in-law said, ``My mother-in-law should be prayed for, and I have prepared for my thoughts.''" and, similar to Owarishi, it is said that the names of upper and lower are the names of places in the Motomiya of Chiba-go. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railway|Q30850|qid=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}{{ill|lt=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|Q1137838|qid=Q1137838|lt=Nagoya Main Line|1=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line}} Get off at {{ill|lt=Honhoshizaki Station|Q6917933|qid=Q6917933|1=Moto Hoshizaki Station}} and walk for about 10 minutes. === Related shrines === === Kegutsuchisha === Address: 726 Terasaka, Honhoshizaki-cho, Minami-ku, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture Enshrined deity: (unknown) Former Mukakusha. Also called Akibasha. The main enshrined deity is thought to be {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Kigutsuchi no Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}. It is enshrined on Mikoshiyama to the west of Kasadera Elementary School, where prayers for Taira no Masakado's death were held. Mikoshiyama is also referred to as ``Mikoshizuka'' and ``Shichimenzuka'' in the Owari Makiyuki. It is said that {{ill|lt=Hojokai|Q11078912|qid=Q11078912|1=Life release}} has been held here to tell the story of the past. There is no mention in the Owari Makiyuki that there is a shrine on that mountain. The company name is mentioned in the Aichi County Magazine published in the Taisho era. === Kanjosha === Address: 146 Hoshizaki 1-chome, Minami-ku, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture Deities enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu no Okami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunijotachison|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Keirigison|1=UNKNOWN}} Before the town name was changed, the address was ``463 Tonokaido, Hoshizaki-cho.'' Old village company. It is also referred to as ``Shinmei Shrine'' in the Owari Makiyuki and ``Yotsugu Shinmeisha'' in the Owari Chronicles. The Minamino meteorite is considered a sacred object. === Shichisho Shrine === Address: 1-24 Kasutake-cho, Minami-ku, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Nippon Takeru|UNKNOWN|lt=Nippon Takeru}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazakihime|Q11455216|qid=Q11455216|lt=Miyazakihime no Mikoto|1=Miyazu-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uga Mitama no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, { {jalink|Nostopyo-mikoto|Q97706258}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susa-no-o-mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amanohinomi-mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaninho-no-mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} Old town company. It is also referred to as ``Shichisho Myo Shrine'' and ``Shichisho Shrine'' in the Owari Makiyuki. It is said that during the {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|qid=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} years (938 - 947), the prayer for the Taira no Masakado chofu held at Hoshizaki had divine power, so he solicited the Atsuta Seven Shrines to pray for the peace of the nation. {{ill|lt=Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Journal Materials|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|lt=Meiji Shrine Journal Materials|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}} notes that the year of its founding was {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193549|qid=Q1193549|1=Eishō (Heian period)}} years (1046 - 1053) and that it ``looks like a book.'' The Aichi County Magazine says, ``According to the shrine biography, it was a solicitation during the Eisho era.'' === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2|refs= Hoshimiyasha, Minami Ward, Nagoya City. Kumano Sanja Matsuo Island, Minami Ward, Nagoya City. Reference collaborative database. Owari Masayuki. There is an explanation in Minami Ward, Nagoya City, "[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/minami/page/0000001442.html Kantsugi Shrine]" (accessed March 6, 2017). The enshrined deity and the old shrine rank based on the modern shrine rank system are based on the Aichi County Journal. There is an explanation in Minami Ward, Nagoya City "[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/minami/page/0000001708.html Shichisho Shrine]" (accessed March 6, 2017). Refers to Jizo-in (hot spring Jizo) located at 3-11 Yotsugi-cho, Minami-ku, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture. The tradition continues as follows. After the night of July 7th, from the day the morning star fell, the old man told Urato that a Buddhist statue had gone up in Idoda Village, and the place came to be called ``Yotsugu no Hama.'' Since the old man seemed to disappear, it was later thought to be a message from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kasadera Kannon|1=UNKNOWN}}. When villagers poured water into a vessel and washed the Buddha statue, they discovered that it was Jizo Bodhisattva, and this came to be known as ``Jizo of Washing.'' In this tradition as well, the number ``seven days and seven nights'' associated with Myoken belief appears. }} === References === * Minami-ku, Nagoya “[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/minami/page/0000012431.html Hoshinomiyasha]”. Explanation of Hoshimiyasha. Accessed March 6, 2017. * Minami-ku, Nagoya "[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/minami/page/0000001717.html Kumano Sanja Matsuojima]". Explanation of Kumano Sanja Matsuoshima. There is mention of the origin of ``Matsuoshima.'' Accessed March 6, 2017. * {{ill|lt=National Diet Library|Q477675|qid=Q477675|1=National Diet Library}}・{{ill|lt=Reference Collaborative Database|Q11349872|qid=Q11349872|1=Collaborative Reference Database}}・Reference case details “[https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/entry/index.php?page=ref_view&id=1000135055 Has a meteorite ever fallen in Nagoya]”. Answer by {{ill|lt=Nagoya City Tsurumai Central Library|Q707258|qid=Q707258|1=Tsuruma Central Library}} on April 3, 2013. Accessed March 6, 2017. * Yoshifuru Higuchi's ``Owari Kikoki''. The full text can be viewed from [http://www.aichi-pref-library.jp/ Aichi Arts Center Aichi Prefectural Library] and [https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/index.html Rare Japanese Book Digital Library] (pages 50-52 of Volume 3).* ``Owarishi'' by Masaaki Fukada and others. Tenpo 15 (1844). The full text can be viewed from [http://www.aichi-pref-library.jp/ Aichi Arts Center Aichi Prefectural Library], [https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/index.html Rare Japanese Book Digital Library] and {{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collection|Q117959850|qid=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}. * Ryo Ota “Owari”. Taisho 15. Isobe Koyodo, Japanese National Journal Materials Series. Available for viewing from the National Diet Library Digital Collection. * “Aichi County Magazine” edited by Aichi County. Taisho 12. Aichi Gun Publishing. Available for viewing from the National Diet Library Digital Collection. * “Specially Selected Divine Names” edited by the Ministry of Education. Taisho 14. Isobe Koyodo. Available for viewing from the National Diet Library Digital Collection. * {{ill|lt=Owari Famous Places Pictures|Q11465290|qid=Q11465290|lt=``Owari Famous Places Pictures'' Volume 5 Hoshi no Miya|1=Owari meisho zue}}, 1844 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Mr. Owari|Q11465311|qid=Q11465311|1=Owari clan}} === External link === {{osm box|n|5541532636}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Hoshinomiya-sha Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagoya]] [[Category:History of Minami ward]] [[Category:637年]] [[Category:Buildings in Minami ward]] [[Category:Son-sha in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]]
